nativeRange.habitat: Surge zones and reef crests of oceanic islands; strong wave-swept shallow reef flats and outer reef edges.

decorPreferences: Strong turbulent flow simulating surge zones
Pristine water with high dissolved oxygen

diet.notes: Primarily grazes filamentous and small fleshy algae in the wild. In captivity, has been reported to do best with constant access to dried marine algae (nori) clipped in the tank, supplemented with spirulina-rich pellets, vitamin-enriched frozen mysis, and occasional vegetable foods. A vitamin-rich algae-heavy diet is generally considered essential for reducing HLLE risk.

goodWith: Larger peaceful fish of unrelated families
Most reef-safe fish that are not tangs or surgeonfish
avoidWith: Aggressive triggerfish that may bully it during acclimation

imageCandidates.notes: Hectonichus, 2124x1599. Sharp full side profile of a vibrant adult; black body, vivid orange teardrop patch at the tail base, orange/red white-margined caudal fin and blue fin edges all clearly shown.
cello caruso-turiello, 2048x1365. Sharp in-situ reef shot; whole fish in frame with strong colour and clear diagnostic markings.
Thomas Irvine, 1144x687. Very vivid full broadside view in natural habitat; slightly lower resolution but crisp and highly colourful.

summary: The Achilles Tang is a striking black surgeonfish with a vivid orange teardrop patch near the tail, native to surge-swept Pacific reefs. It is widely regarded as one of the most demanding tangs in the marine hobby, requiring expert care, a very large tank, and a consistent algae-rich diet.
careNotes: Generally considered one of the most demanding surgeonfishes in the marine hobby and is widely regarded as expert-only. Wild specimens are found in shallow surge zones of oceanic Pacific reefs, so aquarium setups are commonly advised to provide turbulent flow, high dissolved oxygen, and a long footprint of open swimming space. Has a thin slime coat and is highly susceptible to marine ich and other skin parasites; quarantine and prophylactic treatment have been reported as near-mandatory by experienced keepers. Also prone to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when diet and water quality slip; vitamin-rich algae sheets and frequent feedings have been reported to help prevention. Highly aggressive toward other tangs and conspecifics; generally advised to be the only Acanthurus in the tank. Generally considered reef-safe with corals and invertebrates, though isolated reports of hungry specimens nipping at LPS (large-polyp stony corals) exist. Carries a sharp caudal scalpel on each side of the tail base — should be handled with caution.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page returned 404 on direct fetch; care details extracted from LiveAquaria's indexed product description via search snippets (180-gal minimum, expert care level, aggressive toward other tangs, ich-susceptible, herbivore diet).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes), max length 24 cm TL, depth 0-10 m, temperature 25.8-28.3°C, distribution (Western Pacific oceanic islands to Hawaiian/Pitcairn islands and Eastern Central Pacific), herbivorous diet, monogamous.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (700 L minimum), water parameters (~26°C, SG 1.026), reef-safe, aggressive toward food competitors, primarily algae feeder.

additional.name: Aquarium Specialty
additional.notes: Cross-checked 180-gal (~680 L) minimum, expert-only difficulty, ich susceptibility, herbivore diet with seaweed/marine algae primary.

additional.name: TFH Magazine
additional.notes: Cross-checked HLLE and ich susceptibility, aggression toward other tangs/triggers, generally reef-safe with occasional LPS-nipping caveat, surge-zone habitat. Reef2Reef wiki returned 403; substituted TFH as additional secondary.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Requires a very large (700+ L) tank with surge-zone flow, pristine water, and a relentless algae-rich feeding regimen. Highly susceptible to ich and HLLE; generally considered one of the most demanding surgeonfishes and unsuited to typical home aquarists.
